skills.aiInstallPrompt:
Install the skill 'chart-recommend' by writing the file below.

Universal path: ./skills/chart-recommend/SKILL.md
Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/chart-recommend/SKILL.md
Cursor path: ~/.cursor/skills/chart-recommend/SKILL.md
Claude path: ~/.claude/skills/chart-recommend/SKILL.md
VS Code agent path: .github/skills/chart-recommend/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: chart-recommend
description: >
  Recommend best chart type and spec for data. Triggers: "chart recommend", /chart-recommend.
metadata:
  category: data
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /chart-recommend

Recommend best chart type and spec for data.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

User has data (or a description of data) and needs to know which chart to build - for a dashboard, slide, report, or one-off analysis - and how to spec it.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- The data itself, or its shape: what variables, what types (categorical, numeric, dates), how many categories/points/series
- The question the chart must answer ("did X grow?", "which segment is biggest?", "are these related?")
- Audience and medium: exec slide, live dashboard, print report, social post
- Any tooling constraints (Excel, Google Sheets, Tableau, matplotlib, BI tool)

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Analytical, show your work, call out data quality issues.
- **Audience:** Analyst or decision-maker.

## Workflow

1. **Classify the data shape and the question.** Match to one of these patterns before naming any chart:
   - **Comparison across categories** → bar chart (horizontal if labels are long or there are >7 categories); grouped bars only up to ~3 series, otherwise small multiples
   - **Change over time** → line chart for continuous trends; bar for discrete periods (e.g., quarterly totals); area only when the cumulative total itself matters
   - **Distribution of one variable** → histogram or box plot; violin/density if audience is technical; box plots for comparing distributions across groups
   - **Correlation / relationship between two numerics** → scatter plot; add trend line only if a relationship genuinely exists; bubble size for a careful third variable
   - **Part-of-whole** → stacked bar or 100% stacked bar; pie only with ≤5 slices and one clearly dominant share; treemap for many nested parts
   - **Ranking** → sorted horizontal bar; slope chart or dumbbell for rank change between two points in time
   - **Flow / movement between states** → Sankey or waterfall (waterfall for additive breakdowns like revenue bridges)
   - **Geographic** → choropleth for rates (never raw counts - population confounds), symbol map for magnitudes at points
2. **Pick the single best chart and one alternative.** State a one-line justification tied to the question ("Line, because the question is trend, not level"). Offer exactly one runner-up with the tradeoff.
3. **Spec it.** Axes (which variable where, units, whether y starts at zero), sort order, series limit, color use (one highlight color, gray for context), labeling (direct-label lines instead of a legend when ≤4 series), and a title written as the takeaway ("Churn doubled after March pricing change"), not a description ("Churn over time").
4. **Flag mistakes this specific chart invites.** Draw from the common-error list below and name only the ones relevant to the recommendation.
5. **Note data-quality caveats** - missing periods, tiny sample in one category, mixed units - that would make even the right chart misleading.

## Output format

- **Recommendation** - chart type in bold + one-line justification tied to the user's question
- **Spec** - bullet list: x-axis, y-axis (and whether zero-baseline is required), sort order, series/slice count, color and labeling guidance, takeaway-style title suggestion
- **Alternative** - one runner-up chart and when it would win instead
- **What to avoid** - the specific pitfalls for this data, chosen from: pie charts with >5 slices; dual y-axes (implies false correlation - use two stacked panels instead); truncated y-axis on bar charts (bars encode length, so zero baseline is mandatory; lines may zoom, with a note); 3D anything; rainbow categorical palettes; smoothing that hides real volatility; cumulative charts that only ever go up; stacked areas where middle-series trends become unreadable
- **Data caveats** - anything about the data itself the reader should know before trusting the chart

## Do

- Ask at most ONE clarifying question if critical info is missing - the question the chart must answer is the one worth asking
- Recommend the boring chart when it wins; bar and line beat novel chart types for most audiences
- Adapt the spec to the user's stated tool (e.g., "in Excel: Insert → 2-D Bar, then sort source data descending")
- Deliver copy-paste-ready output

## Don't

- Don't invent facts the user didn't provide, including fake sample data values
- Don't recommend a chart the data can't support (e.g., a trend line for two time points)
- Don't offer a menu of six chart types - commit to one plus one alternative
- Don't over-explain visualization theory - show the recommendation

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The recommendation names one specific chart type, not a category ("bar", not "some kind of comparison chart")
- [ ] Justification references the user's actual question and data shape
- [ ] Y-axis baseline guidance is explicit if a bar chart is involved
- [ ] Every "avoid" item is relevant to this data, not a generic list
- [ ] User could hand the spec to someone else and get the intended chart back
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /chart-recommend or describe the task from the skill description.

# /csv-clean

Clean messy CSV: headers, types, duplicates.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the user needs a copy-ready workflow for: Clean messy CSV: headers, types, duplicates..

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Data source or question to answer
- Decision the output supports
- Constraints and desired depth

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Analytical, show your work, call out data quality issues.
- **Audience:** Analyst or decision-maker.

## Workflow

1. Inspect file.
2. Fix headers and types.
3. Remove duplicates.
4. Output clean file via xlsx skill if needed.

## Output format

**Issues found**
**Fixes applied**
**Clean schema**
**Sample rows** (before → after)

## Do

- Ask at most ONE clarifying question if critical info is missing
- Deliver copy-paste-ready output

## Don't

- Don't invent facts the user didn't provide
- Don't over-explain the process - show the result

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
```

# /pivot-summary

Summarize tabular data with pivot-style insights.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

Use this when the user needs a copy-ready workflow for: Summarize tabular data with pivot-style insights..

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Source material or data provided
- Decision or question to answer
- Constraints and assumptions
- Desired depth (quick vs thorough)

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Analytical, show your work, call out data quality issues.
- **Audience:** Analyst or decision-maker.

## Workflow

1. Gather inputs: data, context, constraints.
2. Analyze systematically with clear structure.
3. Present findings with recommendations.
4. Flag assumptions and limitations.

## Output format

**Executive summary** → **Findings** (bullets) → **Recommendations** → **Assumptions & limits**

## Do

- Ask at most ONE clarifying question if critical info is missing
- Deliver copy-paste-ready output

## Don't

- Don't invent facts the user didn't provide
- Don't over-explain the process - show the result

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
```

Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/survey-analyze/SKILL.md
Cursor path: ~/.cursor/skills/survey-analyze/SKILL.md

VS Code agent path: .github/skills/survey-analyze/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: survey-analyze
description: >
  Analyze survey responses open and closed. Triggers: "survey analyze", /survey-analyze.
metadata:
  category: data
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /survey-analyze

Analyze survey responses open and closed.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

User has survey results - CSV export, pasted responses, or a tool summary - with closed questions (ratings, multiple choice, NPS) and/or open-text answers, and needs findings they can report.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- The response data itself (raw rows preferred over pre-aggregated summaries)
- The questionnaire: exact question wording and answer options
- How many people were invited vs. how many responded
- Who was surveyed and how they were recruited (all customers? a mailing list? social post?)
- Any segments that matter to the user (plan tier, region, tenure, role)
- The decision this survey is supposed to inform

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Analytical, show your work, call out data quality issues.
- **Audience:** Analyst or decision-maker.

## Workflow

1. **Sanity-check the responses before analyzing.** Compute response rate (responses ÷ invited) if invite count is known. Check for: duplicate submissions, straight-lining (same rating on every question), near-empty responses, and suspicious completion patterns. State n for the survey and for every question (drop-off means later questions have smaller n).
2. **Assess representativeness.** Who could answer this survey, and who actually did? Name the likely self-selection bias in one sentence (e.g., "opt-in feedback surveys over-represent the very happy and very angry"). If respondent demographics are available, compare against the known population.
3. **Tabulate closed questions.** For each: counts and percentages per option, with n. For rating scales, report the distribution (or top-2-box / bottom-2-box), not just the mean - a mean of 3.0 from all-3s and from half-1s-half-5s are different findings. For NPS, show promoter/passive/detractor counts alongside the score.
4. **Code open-text answers into themes.** Read through, derive 4-8 themes from the actual responses (don't impose a pre-made list), tag each response with one or more themes, and count. Keep an "other/unclear" bucket rather than force-fitting. Pull 1-3 short verbatim quotes per theme, quoted exactly from the user's data.
5. **Cut by segment where it changes the story.** Run the key questions by the user's segments. Report a segment difference only when the subgroup n supports it; with small subgroups, describe direction ("skews lower among trial users, n=12") rather than precise percentages.
6. **Attach statistical caution notes where warranted, not as boilerplate.** Flag: small n (rough guide: treat percentage differences on n<30 subgroups as anecdote); differences within the margin of noise for the sample size; leading or double-barreled question wording that taints a result; comparisons to a previous survey with different wording or audience.
7. **Answer the user's actual decision question last**, drawing only on findings the data supports, and state what this survey cannot tell them.

## Output format

- **Survey at a glance** - n, response rate (if computable), field dates if known, one-line representativeness caveat
- **Findings by question** - one block per question:
  - Question text (verbatim)
  - For closed questions: a small table of option / count / % (with n stated)
  - For open questions: a **theme table** - theme / count / % of coders' n / 1-2 representative verbatim quotes
  - One-line takeaway per question
- **Segment cuts** - only the cuts that revealed a real difference, each with subgroup n
- **Caution notes** - the specific statistical caveats for this dataset (small n, self-selection, drop-off, wording issues)
- **So what** - 2-4 bullets connecting findings to the user's decision, each traceable to a question above

## Do

- Ask at most ONE clarifying question if critical info is missing - invited-vs-responded count is usually the one worth asking
- State n next to every percentage
- Quote open-text responses verbatim; mark themes as your coding, not respondents' words
- Deliver copy-paste-ready output

## Don't

- Don't invent facts the user didn't provide - no fabricated quotes, no assumed demographics, no made-up benchmarks ("industry average NPS is…")
- Don't report percentages to decimal places on small samples - false precision
- Don't treat an opt-in survey as if it measures the whole customer base
- Don't over-explain methodology - show the findings

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every percentage has an n beside or near it
- [ ] All quotes are verbatim from the provided responses
- [ ] Themes were derived from the data, and the "other" bucket exists
- [ ] At least one concrete bias/caution note specific to this survey, not generic
- [ ] The "so what" bullets each trace back to a tabulated finding
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /survey-analyze or describe the task from the skill description.
skills.skillContent:
---
name: survey-analyze
description: >
  Analyze survey responses open and closed. Triggers: "survey analyze", /survey-analyze.
metadata:
  category: data
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /survey-analyze

Analyze survey responses open and closed.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

User has survey results - CSV export, pasted responses, or a tool summary - with closed questions (ratings, multiple choice, NPS) and/or open-text answers, and needs findings they can report.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- The response data itself (raw rows preferred over pre-aggregated summaries)
- The questionnaire: exact question wording and answer options
- How many people were invited vs. how many responded
- Who was surveyed and how they were recruited (all customers? a mailing list? social post?)
- Any segments that matter to the user (plan tier, region, tenure, role)
- The decision this survey is supposed to inform

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Analytical, show your work, call out data quality issues.
- **Audience:** Analyst or decision-maker.

## Workflow

1. **Sanity-check the responses before analyzing.** Compute response rate (responses ÷ invited) if invite count is known. Check for: duplicate submissions, straight-lining (same rating on every question), near-empty responses, and suspicious completion patterns. State n for the survey and for every question (drop-off means later questions have smaller n).
2. **Assess representativeness.** Who could answer this survey, and who actually did? Name the likely self-selection bias in one sentence (e.g., "opt-in feedback surveys over-represent the very happy and very angry"). If respondent demographics are available, compare against the known population.
3. **Tabulate closed questions.** For each: counts and percentages per option, with n. For rating scales, report the distribution (or top-2-box / bottom-2-box), not just the mean - a mean of 3.0 from all-3s and from half-1s-half-5s are different findings. For NPS, show promoter/passive/detractor counts alongside the score.
4. **Code open-text answers into themes.** Read through, derive 4-8 themes from the actual responses (don't impose a pre-made list), tag each response with one or more themes, and count. Keep an "other/unclear" bucket rather than force-fitting. Pull 1-3 short verbatim quotes per theme, quoted exactly from the user's data.
5. **Cut by segment where it changes the story.** Run the key questions by the user's segments. Report a segment difference only when the subgroup n supports it; with small subgroups, describe direction ("skews lower among trial users, n=12") rather than precise percentages.
6. **Attach statistical caution notes where warranted, not as boilerplate.** Flag: small n (rough guide: treat percentage differences on n<30 subgroups as anecdote); differences within the margin of noise for the sample size; leading or double-barreled question wording that taints a result; comparisons to a previous survey with different wording or audience.
7. **Answer the user's actual decision question last**, drawing only on findings the data supports, and state what this survey cannot tell them.

## Output format

- **Survey at a glance** - n, response rate (if computable), field dates if known, one-line representativeness caveat
- **Findings by question** - one block per question:
  - Question text (verbatim)
  - For closed questions: a small table of option / count / % (with n stated)
  - For open questions: a **theme table** - theme / count / % of coders' n / 1-2 representative verbatim quotes
  - One-line takeaway per question
- **Segment cuts** - only the cuts that revealed a real difference, each with subgroup n
- **Caution notes** - the specific statistical caveats for this dataset (small n, self-selection, drop-off, wording issues)
- **So what** - 2-4 bullets connecting findings to the user's decision, each traceable to a question above

## Do

- Ask at most ONE clarifying question if critical info is missing - invited-vs-responded count is usually the one worth asking
- State n next to every percentage
- Quote open-text responses verbatim; mark themes as your coding, not respondents' words
- Deliver copy-paste-ready output

## Don't

- Don't invent facts the user didn't provide - no fabricated quotes, no assumed demographics, no made-up benchmarks ("industry average NPS is…")
- Don't report percentages to decimal places on small samples - false precision
- Don't treat an opt-in survey as if it measures the whole customer base
- Don't over-explain methodology - show the findings

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every percentage has an n beside or near it
- [ ] All quotes are verbatim from the provided responses
- [ ] Themes were derived from the data, and the "other" bucket exists
- [ ] At least one concrete bias/caution note specific to this survey, not generic
- [ ] The "so what" bullets each trace back to a tabulated finding

skills.aiInstallPrompt:
Install the skill 'survey-design' by writing the file below.

Universal path: ./skills/survey-design/SKILL.md
Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/survey-design/SKILL.md
Cursor path: ~/.cursor/skills/survey-design/SKILL.md
Claude path: ~/.claude/skills/survey-design/SKILL.md
VS Code agent path: .github/skills/survey-design/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: survey-design
description: >
  Design survey questions with bias check. Triggers: "survey design", /survey-design.
metadata:
  category: data
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /survey-design

Design survey questions with bias check.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when the user needs a copy-ready workflow for: Design survey questions with bias check..

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; only ask if missing:

- Research goal and target respondents
- Survey length target
- Distribution channel

## Tone & voice

- **Tone:** Analytical, show your work, call out data quality issues.
- **Audience:** Analyst or decision-maker.

## Workflow

1. Define research objective and hypotheses.
2. Draft screener, core questions, and closing.
3. Check each question for leading/biased wording.
4. Recommend question types and order.

## Output format

**Objective & hypotheses**
**Audience & length**
**Screener** (if needed)
**Core questions** (type, wording, why)
**Bias check notes**
**Closing & thank-you**
**Analysis plan**

## Do

- Ask at most ONE clarifying question if critical info is missing
- Deliver copy-paste-ready output

## Don't

- Don't invent facts the user didn't provide
- Don't over-explain the process - show the result

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Output matches requested tone and channel
- [ ] No placeholder brackets left unfilled (use sensible defaults or ask)
- [ ] User can copy-paste the result immediately
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /survey-design or describe the task from the skill description.
